title: What Building a Social Scheduler Taught Me About Reliability
body:
Most automation tools feel impressive right up until you have to trust them.

It is easy to demo a scheduler.

It is much harder to build one that survives real life:
- expired tokens
- flaky platform APIs
- posts that need review
- queues that drift out of sync
- failures that happen while you are asleep

That gap changed how I think about automation.

I have been building a project called PostPunk, and I thought the hard part would be generating content and wiring platform APIs together. That turned out to be the easy part.

The real work was operational trust.

A useful scheduler needs a few things that demos usually skip:

## 1. Approval states matter

Not every post should go straight from draft to published.

Some posts are written by you and are ready immediately. Some are AI-assisted and need a second look. If your system does not distinguish that clearly, you end up either babysitting everything or accidentally posting junk.

So I ended up treating post states as part of the core workflow, not just metadata:
- draft
- approved
- posted
- failed

That sounds simple, but it changes everything.

## 2. Failure is not an edge case

In scheduling systems, failure is normal.

A platform token expires.
An API rate-limits you.
A platform changes a field name.
An account is misconfigured.
A browser fallback stops matching the UI.

If the whole system falls apart the moment one target fails, you do not have automation. You have a fragile demo.

So the system needs:
- retries
- visible failure states
- alerts
- manual assist when automation is not trustworthy

## 3. Automatic is not the same as unattended

A lot of posting tools sell the dream of full autopilot. In practice, a better model is often:
- automate what is stable
- assist where platforms are hostile
- keep the queue and schedule as the source of truth

That still saves a huge amount of time, and more importantly, it is honest.

## 4. Scheduling is really an operations problem

The UI part is not enough.

You also need:
- a worker that checks for due posts
- logs
- retry logic
- success and failure alerts
- a clear what is due today view

Without those, the schedule is just decorative.

## What I learned

The hardest part of automation is not getting software to do something once.

It is getting it to behave predictably enough that you stop worrying about it.

That is the part I care about now.

If you are building internal tools, creator tools, or any kind of scheduler, reliability is the actual feature. Everything else is packaging.

PostPunk is still a project I am actively working on, but building it has made me much less interested in shiny automation promises and much more interested in boring operational trust.

title: The best prompt packs do not make you feel smarter. They make you start.
body:
A lot of creative tools overpromise genius and underdeliver momentum.

100 Prompt Storm is useful because it reduces friction at the exact point most people stall: the blank page.

That matters more than hype.

title: Founders do not need more ideas. They need better filters.
body:
Most founders are not stuck because they lack ideas. They are stuck because they keep feeding time into the wrong ones.

Product Strategy 25 is built to force sharper decisions earlier so you waste less time building things no one wants.

If your product backlog feels like a junk drawer, this is for you.

title: Most Founders Do Not Need More Ideas. They Need Better Filters
body:
Founders do not need more ideas. They need better filters.

A lot of builders are not blocked because they lack creativity.

They are blocked because every idea feels plausible for just long enough to steal a week.

That is how backlogs turn into junk drawers.

You start with ambition:
- new feature ideas
- growth experiments
- AI angles
- platform expansions
- side bets that might be big

Pretty soon, you are not building a strategy anymore. You are just collecting options.

## The real problem is idea inflation

Every new idea arrives with a story:
- this could unlock growth
- this could differentiate the product
- this could bring in a new audience
- this could be quick to ship

Without a filter, every idea gets treated like an opportunity instead of a cost.

That is where founders lose time.

## A better question

Instead of asking Is this a good idea, ask:
- Is this aligned with the current product goal?
- Does this solve a painful problem for the intended buyer?
- Is this more important than what is already in flight?
- What do we stop doing if we do this?
- What evidence do we have besides excitement?

Excitement is not strategy.

## Why this is hard

Filtering sounds obvious, but it gets harder when:
- you are building alone
- you have multiple product directions
- you can execute fast
- new tools make every idea feel easier than it really is

Speed makes weak prioritization more dangerous, not less.

You can waste a month very efficiently.

## What helped me

I started treating product decisions less like inspiration and more like a sorting problem.

Not every good idea belongs in the current season of the business.

Some ideas are:
- now
- later
- maybe
- not for this audience
- not worth the maintenance cost

That framing removes a lot of guilt. You do not need to kill every idea. You just need to stop letting all of them compete for today.

## Final thought

The founders I trust most are not the ones with the most ideas.

They are the ones who can explain why they are ignoring ten attractive ones in order to finish one thing that matters.

That is the skill.

If you want a sharper way to evaluate, sort, and pressure-test product ideas, I made this for exactly that problem:
https://fleurdevie.gumroad.com/l/product-strategy-25

title: Prompt Packs Are Not Magic. They Are Starting Systems
body:
Blank pages kill more good products than bad ideas do.

A lot of creators and founders do not stall because they have nothing to say.

They stall because starting is expensive.

You open the document.
You know the product.
You know the audience.
You even know you should be posting.

But your brain still gives you static.

That is why prompt packs exist.

Not because they are magic.
Because they reduce the cost of starting.

## The wrong expectation

People often expect prompts to do all the thinking for them.

That is the fastest way to get generic content.

A useful prompt is not a substitute for judgment. It is a starting structure:
- a direction
- an angle
- a constraint
- a way to get moving

That is enough more often than people think.

## Why starting matters so much

Once a draft exists, it gets easier.

You can improve weak writing.
You can tighten a hook.
You can change the tone.
You can throw out half of it and keep the good line.

But if nothing exists, none of that happens.

The real bottleneck is often activation, not capability.

## What a good prompt system does

A good prompt system should help you:
- find angles faster
- match the content to the platform
- avoid repeating the same post over and over
- get from idea to rough draft without burning an hour

That is the difference between prompt entertainment and an actual workflow tool.

## My rule for using prompts

I like prompts that give me one of three things:
- perspective
- structure
- momentum

If a prompt does not help with one of those, it usually is not useful enough.

## Final thought

The best prompts do not replace your voice.

They help you reach it sooner.

That is the point.

If you want a starting system for content ideas, angles, and momentum instead of a pile of random prompt fluff, this is the one I made:
https://fleurdevie.gumroad.com/l/100prompt-storm
